content.text: The style Prop: Object, Not String
prop accepts a JavaScript object, never a CSS string. This is one of the most common trips for developers coming from HTML: in HTML you write
, but in JSX you write an object with camelCase keys.
The double braces are not special syntax — the outer braces open a JSX expression and the inner braces open a JavaScript object literal. You can also pass the object by reference:

const cardStyle = { color: 'red', fontSize: 14 };
<div style={cardStyle}>  // single braces — the expression evaluates to the object

attrs.alt: style prop: syntax rules, dynamic patterns, and limitations

content.text: Every CSS property that contains a hyphen becomes camelCase in the style object. Single-word properties (
Vendor-prefixed properties use an uppercase first letter after the prefix: e.g.,
prefix and hyphens and must be quoted as string keys.
For CSS properties that accept length values, React automatically appends the pixel unit to bare numbers. This means
in the DOM. For other numeric properties like
(when unitless), the number is used directly.

<div style={{
  fontSize: 16,            // → font-size: 16px
  lineHeight: 1.5,         // → line-height: 1.5 (unitless — intentional)
  opacity: 0.8,            // → opacity: 0.8
  zIndex: 100,             // → z-index: 100
  borderWidth: 1,          // → border-width: 1px
  width: '50%',            // → width: 50%  (string — keeps as-is)
  padding: '8px 16px',     // → padding: 8px 16px
  margin: '1rem auto',     // → margin: 1rem auto
}}>
Pass a number when the value is in pixels. Pass a string that includes the unit for any other unit (rem, em, %, vw, etc.).
Because the style prop is a JavaScript object, any property can be computed at render time using the full power of JavaScript expressions.
Computed style objects keep your logic in JavaScript and avoid the overhead of toggling CSS classes for small, data-driven variations.
CSS custom properties (variables) can be set through the
and must be quoted because they are hyphenated and thus not valid JavaScript identifiers.
This pattern lets you pass dynamic values from JavaScript into the CSS cascade without needing inline styles on every consumer. Child components read the variable with
To combine a base style with an override, use object spread. The last key wins for duplicates:
is a no-op spread — spreading a falsy value adds nothing to the object. This pattern avoids conditional property checks inside the object literal.
Creating a new object literal inline creates a new reference every render. For most components this does not matter, but for children wrapped in
, a new style object reference every render defeats the shallow comparison that memo relies on. Extract static style objects to module scope or stabilise dynamic ones with
For components that are not wrapped in
, inline style objects are fine — the performance cost of creating a small object is negligible. Only extract to constants or
when memo is involved or profiling shows an actual issue.
Inline styles cannot express several CSS features that stylesheets can:
, etc., through the style prop. Use a CSS class with these selectors.
rules are not possible inline. Use CSS classes, CSS Modules, or a CSS-in-JS library.
live in stylesheets. The inline style can control the
properties, but the keyframe definition itself needs a stylesheet.
) are not possible. Each element must be styled individually.
Dynamic, data-driven styles that change per-component instance (position, size from state, theme colors) or values that are computed in JS. For static and interactive styles, prefer CSS classes.
, which is an interface mapping every CSS property to its allowed value type. This means autocomplete works for property names and TypeScript catches invalid value types (e.g.,
catches typos in property names and incorrect value types before runtime. You also get IDE autocomplete for all CSS properties.
Inline Styles vs CSS Modules vs CSS-in-JS
Choose the right tool for the situation:
Dynamic values require inline style or CSS vars

A common pattern is CSS Modules for layout, typography, and interactive states, combined with inline styles only for the data-driven values that change per instance (position, size, theme tokens via CSS variables).

The style prop accepts a JavaScript object with camelCase property names. Numbers are treated as pixels for length properties; non-pixel units require string values. CSS custom properties retain their hyphenated form and must be quoted. Inline styles excel at per-instance dynamic values but cannot express pseudo-selectors, media queries, or keyframe animations — for those, use CSS classes or a stylesheet.
Transitions and Animations with Inline Styles
property works with inline styles to animate between values. Setting it as a string lets you control which properties animate, their duration, easing, and delay.
React does not add vendor prefixes to transition values automatically. Write the CSS value string directly — modern browsers support unprefixed transitions.
rule to a stylesheet and control the animation properties through the inline style. This splits the definition (CSS) from the data-driven application (inline style).
CSS transforms compose by concatenating multiple transform functions in a single string. Specifying
twice in the same object means the second value wins and the first is silently discarded — build the string manually to compose transforms.

Inline styles serialise correctly for server-side rendering. React converts the style object to an inline style string in the HTML output, so there is no flash of unstyled content for server-rendered style values. CSS class names require the stylesheet to be loaded before they apply, but inline styles are embedded in the HTML directly and available immediately.
CSS inheritance means some properties (color, font-size, line-height) cascade from parent elements. You can reset them inline with the values
Set CSS custom properties on a root element from JS state to bridge dynamic values into the CSS cascade, rather than passing inline styles to every leaf node.
Use inline style for computed values that change based on JavaScript state (position, opacity, size from measurements) and CSS classes for interactive states.
Do not copy styles that never change into inline style objects; they add per-render allocation and bypass the browser's CSS caching.
Anti-pattern — complex style logic in JSX:
When style logic grows large, extract it to a helper function. Keep the JSX readable.
Anti-pattern — functions or objects as values:
Style values must be strings or numbers. A function or plain object value produces a React warning and unexpected output.
React Testing Library exposes inline styles through the element's
accepts either an object (like the style prop) or a CSS string. It checks the computed style on the element, which for inline styles matches exactly what was passed.

Practice: take a component that uses conditional class toggling for size and color, and refactor the data-driven parts to inline styles while keeping interactive state styles (hover, focus) in CSS classes.
